objective.subject: Mathematics
objective.statement: Interpret, construct and critique tables, bar charts and line graphs by choosing suitable axes, scales, labels and evidence-based comparisons.
misconceptions: Reads a graph by bar height or line direction without checking the axis scale.
Chooses an uneven or unlabelled scale that changes the meaning of the data.
Confuses table rows and columns, so matches values to the wrong category.
Treats every line graph as showing a straight-line rule rather than connected measurements over an ordered scale.
Makes a claim from a graph without citing the exact data that supports it.
objective.parent_explanation: Can read and build graphs and tables carefully, including axes, scales and evidence-based claims.
objective.teacher_evidence: Chooses suitable graph structures, reads non-unit scales accurately, repairs misleading axes and supports interpretations with exact data values.

activities.title: I can read, build and repair graphs and tables so the data tells the truth clearly.
activities.prompt: Build the axes before plotting: title, labels, units and equal scale steps.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Match each table value to the right bar on the graph.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check that learners connect categories, rows and values before interpreting patterns.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Read the category first, then follow across the row to its value.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A two-column table and a bar chart light up matching category-value pairs.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build the axes before plotting: title, labels, units and equal scale steps.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Make graph construction a set of deliberate design decisions rather than decoration.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A trustworthy graph needs labelled axes, units and a scale with equal steps.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: An empty graph frame gains axis labels, unit badges and evenly spaced tick marks.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The vertical scale counts in 5s. What value does this point show?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model careful scale reading before answering from graph position.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: First inspect the scale. Each tick is five, so halfway between ten and fifteen is twelve point five if the graph allows decimals.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Tick intervals are counted aloud before a reading ruler aligns with the data point.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Repair this misleading graph by choosing an even scale and adding missing labels.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise diagnosing uneven intervals, missing units and compressed or exaggerated patterns.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A repaired graph uses equal gaps for equal number changes and tells the reader what each axis measures.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Uneven ticks straighten into equal intervals while warning markers turn into label prompts.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which claim is supported by the graph, and which exact values prove it?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check evidence-based interpretation rather than impressionistic graph reading.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A graph claim should point to the data values that support it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Claim cards connect to highlighted bars, points or table cells.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why a graph with uneven scale steps can make a small change look huge.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Consolidate scale fairness and reader trust through explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I made the gaps different sizes to fit the data. Why might that mislead the reader?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion compares a misleading graph with a repaired graph using the same data.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A labelled line on a graph that shows what is being measured.
vocabulary.audio_script: An axis tells us what the numbers or categories on a graph mean.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A glowing horizontal and vertical axis with label tags.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The regular steps used on an axis, such as counting in 2s, 5s or 10s.
vocabulary.audio_script: A scale uses equal steps so values can be read fairly.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Equal tick marks stretching along a graph axis.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An ordered pair that locates a point, usually written x first and y second.
vocabulary.audio_script: A coordinate gives the x value first, then the y value.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A point found by moving across, then up.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A general pattern in data, such as increasing, decreasing or staying similar.
vocabulary.audio_script: A trend is the overall pattern, not just one point.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A soft arrow following the shape of plotted data.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A value that is noticeably different from the others.
vocabulary.audio_script: An outlier sits away from the main pattern in the data.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: One data point standing apart from a cluster.

manipulative.purpose: Support graph and table reading by linking rows, columns, categories, plotted points, bars and evidence statements.
manipulative.interaction_notes: Learners scan tables with row-column focus, read graph values with a ruler cursor, compare categories, trace line-graph trends, select evidence for claims and switch between table and graph views.
manipulative.accessibility_notes: Keyboard and switch access expose each data cell, bar and point in a predictable order; audio labels read axis title, unit, tick value and selected data; high-contrast patterns, data sonification, simple text mode and static table fallback reduce dependence on colour, drag and motion.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm data-board theme, stable focus outlines and no flashing warning states or rapid graph transitions.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace animated plotting, snapping and trend tracing with static before-after panels and numbered build steps.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read graph title, axis labels, units, scale interval and selected data values in a consistent order, with replay for each bar, point or cell.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Offer simple text tables, line focus, glossary popovers, short prompts and symbol-supported labels for axis, scale, category and value.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Reveal one decision at a time: read table, inspect axis, choose scale, plot/read value, then make a claim.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow a graph-reading ruler, tick-count checklist, table focus mode and extra wait time before submitting.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Preserve correct data reading as partial success and route only the failed axis, scale or evidence decision to repair.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach axis, scale, interval, value, category, trend, outlier, evidence and compare with visual glossary cards.
adaptive_support.switch_access: Provide linear scan order across table cells, axis controls, graph marks and answer cards, with no task requiring drag-only input.
adaptive_support.simple_text: Every graph item includes a text-table equivalent and a concise description of axis labels, scale and selected marks.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Match table entries to bars, points and categories before making comparisons.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: small data table + linked bar or line graph + selected row/column/value prompts + spoken-reader metadata
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use age-appropriate school, weather, sports and science contexts with all data supplied in the item.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose titles, axis labels, units and graph type for data tables.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: context + data table + graph frame + component bank for title, x-axis, y-axis, units and graph type
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not reward decorative titles over mathematically useful labels; include unit-free category axes and measured value axes.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Read values from non-unit scales and repair graphs with uneven, missing or unsuitable scale intervals.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: data range + candidate scales + misleading graph fault + repair explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Balance scales in 2s, 5s, 10s and 20s; avoid tiny visual differences that cannot be accessed by keyboard or screen reader.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Select graph-supported claims and cite the exact values, categories or intervals that prove them.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: bar or line graph + four claim cards + evidence selection + explanation feedback
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Distractors should be plausible visual impressions, not absurd claims; mark claims unsupported if evidence is insufficient.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Plot ordered pairs and interpret connected measurements over time or ordered categories without overgeneralising between points.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: coordinate table + line graph + point plot/read/interpolate-or-not decision
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Signal when interpolation is appropriate; include negative or zero values only after axis reading is secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain quick graph/table fluency through short spaced items.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: scheduler-selected table, axis or graph-reading prompt + one exact response + accessibility metadata
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval untimed by default and support simple text rendering for every item.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Reads a graph by bar height or line direction without checking the axis scale.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner gives the tick count or visual height instead of the scaled value.
repair_sequence: Pause on the axis and read the first three tick labels.
State the interval aloud before reading the bar or point.
Use a graph-reading ruler to align the mark with the scaled value.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner reads a new value correctly from a non-unit scale and names the interval used.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Chooses an uneven or unlabelled scale that changes the meaning of the data.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner accepts equal visual gaps with unequal numerical intervals or omits the measured unit.
repair_sequence: Compare the numeric changes between adjacent tick labels.
Replace uneven labels with a regular interval that fits the largest value.
Add axis labels and units, then reread one value to test the repair.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner repairs a misleading axis and explains why equal intervals matter.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses table rows and columns, so matches values to the wrong category.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects a value from the neighbouring row or uses the header as data.
repair_sequence: Highlight one complete row and read the row label first.
Trace across to the named column using a focus ruler.
Restate the category-value pair before comparing.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner correctly links three fresh table entries to graph marks.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats every line graph as showing a straight-line rule rather than connected measurements over an ordered scale.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner predicts exact unmeasured or future values without evidence from the data or context.
repair_sequence: Identify what each plotted point actually measures.
Separate measured facts from possible estimates.
Choose a cautious trend statement supported by the plotted values.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner writes a supported trend claim and rejects an over-precise prediction.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Makes a claim from a graph without citing the exact data that supports it.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says a claim is true because it 'looks biggest' or 'goes up' without values.
repair_sequence: Attach the claim card to the exact bar, point or table cell.
Read the values that prove or disprove the claim.
Rewrite the claim with data evidence included.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner selects a supported claim and cites at least two exact values.

feedback.companion_prompt: Teach Nova why a graph with uneven scale steps can make a small change look huge.
feedback.parent_summary: Can read and build graphs and tables carefully, including axes, scales and evidence-based claims.
feedback.teacher_evidence: Chooses suitable graph structures, reads non-unit scales accurately, repairs misleading axes and supports interpretations with exact data values.
next_actions: Interleave with ratio tables and coordinate work once axis reading is secure.
Move from small class data sets to science and geography graphs with explicit source context.
Use retrieval for scale intervals and table row-column accuracy before introducing averages or distributions.

body.prompt: The vertical axis counts in 5s. What value is shown by the point above Wednesday?

hints: Check the scale before reading the point.
questions.explanation: The point above Wednesday lines up with 25 on a vertical axis that counts in 5s.

body.prompt: Choose the best y-axis label and scale for a bar chart of how many pupils chose each lunch option.
choices: Label: Number of pupils; scale: 0 to 20 in 2s
Label: Lunch option; scale: Monday to Friday
Label: Pupils' favourite colour; scale: 0 to 10 in 1s

expected_answer.value: Label: Number of pupils; scale: 0 to 20 in 2s

hints: The y-axis should measure the number in each category.
The scale must use equal steps and fit the largest value, 18.
questions.explanation: The y-axis should be labelled Number of pupils, and counting from 0 to 20 in equal steps of 2 fits all the values.

body.prompt: A graph uses y-axis ticks at 0, 4, 5, 15 and 20 with equal visual gaps. What repair makes the scale fair?
choices: Use equal numerical steps such as 0, 5, 10, 15, 20.
Keep the labels because the gaps look tidy.
Remove the y-axis labels so the graph looks simpler.
Start at 15 so the bars look more different.
body.simple_text: The labels increase by uneven amounts even though the spaces are equal.

expected_answer.value: Use equal numerical steps such as 0, 5, 10, 15, 20.

hints: Equal spaces on an axis must represent equal number changes.
Choose a scale with a regular interval.
questions.explanation: A fair axis has equal visual gaps for equal numerical steps, so 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 repairs the uneven scale.

body.prompt: Which claim is supported by the table, and which values prove it?
choices: Coding had the most attendees: 17 is greater than 14, 12 and 9.
Drama had twice as many attendees as Chess.
Art and Coding had the same number of attendees.
Chess was more popular than Art.

expected_answer.value: Coding had the most attendees: 17 is greater than 14, 12 and 9.

hints: Check each claim against the exact values.
A supported claim should include data that proves it.
questions.explanation: Coding has 17 attendees, which is greater than Art's 14, Drama's 12 and Chess's 9.

body.prompt: A plant was measured once each week. Which statement is safest from the line graph data?
choices: The measured height increased from week 1 to week 4.
The plant grew exactly the same amount every day.
The plant was always 18 cm tall.
The graph proves the plant will be 30 cm in week 5.

expected_answer.value: The measured height increased from week 1 to week 4.

hints: Use only what the measured points show.
The graph shows weekly measurements, not every day or the future.
questions.explanation: The measured values rise from 8 cm to 18 cm, but the graph does not prove exact daily growth or a future height.

reward_payload.explanation: Can read and build graphs and tables carefully, including axes, scales and evidence-based claims.
reward_payload.companion_prompt: Teach Nova why a graph with uneven scale steps can make a small change look huge.
